messages.content: script an example of a Ballon Debate as described by Graham Dixon using the subject about news agencies selecting their stories, use Pearsons English workbook B2+ Unit 7 page 56 as a guide

Narrator: Welcome to the Balloon Debate. Today’s participants are representatives from major news agencies. They are hanging on to balloons, and the aim is to convince us why their news agency should be the last to be saved from being dropped off the balloon. Let's hear what they have to say.

Representative 1: Good afternoon, everyone. As a representative from BBC, I can confidently say that we are the best news agency in the world. Our news is unbiased, and we select news stories that are important to the public. We believe that our integrity is our greatest strength, which is why we should be the last to be saved.

Representative 2: While BBC may claim to be unbiased, we all know that they have a pro-Western stance. As a representative from Al-Jazeera, I can assure you that our news is not biased. We believe in giving a voice to the voiceless, and we keep our content fresh and relevant.

Representative 3: I have to disagree with Al-Jazeera's statement. As a representative from Fox News, we are the only news agency that believes in true democracy. Our news caters to all people, regardless of their race or religion. We report on news that truly matters, and we do not shy away from tough topics.

Representative 4: While Fox News may claim to be the voice of the people, they are known to distort the truth and present fake news. As a representative from CNN, we have a long standing history of presenting factual news that is unbiased. We believe that reporting the truth is the highest form of journalism, and we will always stand by that.

Narrator: Thank you for your arguments. It is now up to the audience to decide who they believe should last to be dropped off the balloon. Please vote now.

And there you have it, a balloon debate that highlights the different objectives and opinions that are present in the news industry.

Now structure the above for a B2 English class at a Cambridge English School (CELTA) for a 30 minute Speaking lesson include, Section 1 introduction to topic/context, 2) Oral fluency and communicative function include a short role play model after thinking time, The lesson will be online using zoom and breakout rooms will be used

Section 1: Introduction to topic/context (5 minutes)

The lesson will begin with an introduction to the topic of news agencies selecting their stories. The teacher will share some information about the different news agencies and their approaches to reporting news. This will be followed by a brief discussion where students will be encouraged to share their opinion on the topic. The teacher will then explain the Balloon Debate activity and provide instructions on how it works.

Section 2: Oral fluency and communicative function (20 minutes)

Students will be divided into breakout rooms and assigned a news agency that they will represent in the Balloon Debate. The teacher will provide them with allocated thinking time to prepare their arguments for why their news agency should be the last to be saved from being dropped off the balloon.

Each student will have to present their argument, using appropriate discourse markers, persuasive language, and expressing their opinion clearly and coherently. Once each student has made their argument, the teacher will provide an opportunity for rebuttals and questions from other students of other news agencies.

After all the arguments have been presented, the teacher will initiate a vote to decide which news agency should be the last to be saved from being dropped off the balloon.

The teacher will also conduct a short role-play model to demonstrate the activity to the students.

Section 3: Conclusion and Feedback (5 minutes)

The teacher will provide feedback to the students, highlighting strengths and weaknesses noticed during the activity. The students will be encouraged to share their opinion on how they found the activity, what they learned, and how they can improve their oral fluency in expressing their opinions. The teacher will also provide a summary of the key points learned from the lesson and reiterate any further actions that may be required to improve oral fluency. 

Overall, the lesson will provide an opportunity for the students to practice their oral fluency, use discourse markers, and persuasive language in expressing their opinions in an engaging and stimulating way.
resources and ideas for a PowerPoint presentation for the above

Here are some resources and ideas for a PowerPoint presentation for the above lesson:

1. Introduction: The introduction slide can include a title "Balloon Debate: News Agency Edition" and a brief overview of the lesson topic. This slide can also include a relevant image such as a picture of news agencies' logos or journalists in action.

2. Context: The context slide can provide a background to the news agency industry, including the purpose of news agencies, examples of news agencies, how they select their stories, and their impact on society. This slide can also include any relevant videos or animations of news agencies.

3. Balloon Debate: The Balloon Debate slide can include instructions on how to play the game, including the roles of each student and how to conduct the vote. It's important to emphasize the need for persuasive language and clear communication in the student's arguments.

4. Breakout Rooms: The breakout rooms slide can provide guidelines on how to use breakout rooms on Zoom. This can include a step-by-step guide on how to create, join, and exit breakout rooms and how to use the chat function during the debate.

5. Role Play Model: The role-play model slide can provide a sample conversation between the teacher and a student. This can help students understand how to use discourse markers and persuasive language to make a compelling argument. A video of the teacher demonstrating the activity can also be included.

6. Conclusion and Feedback: The conclusion and feedback slide can recapitulate the key points learned from the lesson, including the importance of oral fluency and clear communication. This slide can provide some prompts for students to share their feedback and thoughts on their learning experience.

In addition to these resources, the PowerPoint presentation can also include other resources such as quotes, graphs, and other relevant images that can help to illustrate the points and spark student engagement.
Provide a 3 minute discussion about how a ballon debate works for a B2 level English class

A balloon debate is a debating game where participants imagine that they are flying in a hot air balloon, which has suddenly become damaged and they need to reduce the weight of the balloon by throwing someone out.

Each participant takes on the role of a character or represents a particular industry or group. The aim of the game is to persuade the others in the balloon that you should be the one to stay on the balloon and not be thrown out.

In the case of the Balloon Debate: News Agency Edition, participants will represent different news organizations and will deliver a persuasive argument as to why their news agency should be the last to be saved.

The arguments included in the debate are important, but equally important are the skills required to express these arguments effectively. In order to be persuasive, students need to use appropriate discourse markers, persuasive language, and express their opinions clearly and coherently.

Ultimately, the objective of the game is to encourage students to think creatively, practice their oral fluency, and to be critically engaged with their topic. 

If you’re looking for a fun and engaging way to improve students’ oral fluency, enhance critical thinking, and promote academic skills, then the Balloon Debate is a fantastic option. It is interactive, competitive, and encourages students to think outside of the limitations associated with conventional debates.
two phrases that can be taught and modelled for the students at a B2 level that they can utilise during their balloon debate

Here are two phrases that can be taught and modeled to B2 level students:

1. "I strongly believe that..." - This phrase shows a high level of conviction and confidence in your argument. It sets the tone for a persuasive argument and can help students showcase their passion about the topic they are defending.

Example: "I strongly believe that BBC should be the last news agency saved from being dropped off the balloon. They have a long-standing reputation for unbiased reporting and have established themselves as the go-to source for accurate, reliable news."

2. "In addition to that..." - This phrase helps bridge ideas or illustrate connections between arguments. It helps students build on their previous point and add weight to their argument.

Example: "In addition to that, Al Jazeera continuously provides a platform for lesser-known stories around the world. They bring attention to issues that most mainstream media outlets choose to ignore, and their reporting often carries a grassroots perspective that captures the truest reflection of events." 

By teaching and modeling these phrases, students are better equipped to convey their arguments more effectively and convey their thoughts clearly and coherently.
give an example of a two-person 1 min for the modeled phrases in explaining a balloon debate:The teacher will also conduct a short role-play model to demonstrate the activity to the students.

Sure, here's an example of a two-person, one-minute roleplay that might be used to explain how to use the modeled phrases in a balloon debate:

Teacher: "Let's demonstrate how to use the modeled phrases in a balloon debate. Let's say that we're in a Balloon Debate: News Agency Edition, and I'm representing Fox News, and you're representing BBC. The prompt is, 'Why should your news agency be saved from being dropped off the balloon last?'. Use 'I strongly believe that...' and 'In addition to that...' in your answer. You have one minute. Go."

Student: "I strongly believe that BBC should be saved from being dropped off the balloon last. Their commitment to unbiased reporting sets them apart from other news agencies and their reputation as a reliable and credible source of news is unparalleled. In addition to that, BBC's coverage of international news stories is top-notch. They prioritize stories that other news agencies might not deem newsworthy, which helps to bring attention to underreported issues."

Teacher: "Great job. You used 'I strongly believe that...' and 'In addition to that...' to connect your ideas and present a convincing argument. Now let's switch roles and try it again."
websites that can provide free graphics for the above PowerPoint

Here are some websites that you can use to find free graphics for your PowerPoint:

1. Unsplash (https://unsplash.com/) - Unsplash offers a wide range of high-quality photos that are free to use and don't require attribution. 

2. Pexels (https://www.pexels.com/) - Pexels is another excellent website that provides free stock photos and videos, all of which are licensed under the Creative Commons Zero (CC0) license.

3. Freepik (https://www.freepik.com/) - Freepik is a great website for finding high-quality graphics and illustrations. They offer a wide range of free graphics, as well as a subscription-based model for premium graphics.

4. Pixabay (https://pixabay.com/) - Pixabay provides over 1.7 million free high-quality images, illustrations, vector graphics, and videos. All content is released under the Creative Commons Zero (CC0) license, which means that you can use and modify them for any purpose.

5. Canva (https://www.canva.com/) - Canva is not just a graphics website, it also offers free templates, design elements, and icons and illustrations. It provides a user-friendly platform to create designs for social media graphics, posters, presentations, and more.

Remember to always check the license agreement for each graphic you use to ensure that you are compliant with the terms of use.

In the above lesson utilise two trips to breakout rooms with student instructions using instruction checking questions and concept checking questions prior to students going to breakout rooms as well as open class feedback prompts for the teacher to use in the main room highlight the opportunities for use of productive and receptive skills practice for the students and the use of teaching meaning, form and pronunciation by the teacher

Sure, here's an idea for how to incorporate two trips to breakout rooms and practice productive and receptive skills:

Trip 1 to Breakout Rooms:

Instructions: Students will be divided into breakout rooms and assigned a news agency to represent in the Balloon Debate. Explain to students that they will have thinking time to prepare their arguments for why their news agency should be the last to be saved. Encourage students to use persuasive language and clear communication in their arguments. Once each student has made their argument, initiate a Q&A session where students can ask questions of other news agencies.

Instruction Checking Questions: "What is the purpose of this breakout room activity?" "What should you do during the thinking time?" "What's the aim of the Q&A session?"

Concept Checking Questions: "What is persuasive language?" "How would you know if your argument is clear?" "How do you ask effective questions?"

Teacher prompt for Open Class Feedback: "How was your experience in the breakout room? Did you feel confident in expressing your ideas? What might you improve in your persuasive argument?"

Practice Opportunities for Students: Productive skills, such as speaking and active listening, as well as receptive skills, such as critical thinking and comprehension.

Teaching Meaning, Form, and Pronunciation: The teacher can model persuasive language and use discourse markers, whilst checking students' understanding of how to use them and how to convey the intended meaning. The teacher can also focus on stress and intonation patterns in persuasive arguments, in order to help students sound more compelling in their delivery.

Trip 2 to Breakout Rooms:

Instructions: Students will be again divided into breakout rooms and meet their peers from the other news agencies. Each student will then be asked to make a balanced critique of one of their opponents' arguments. Once each student has finished, the teacher will provide feedback and guide the discussion towards constructive criticism and positive feedback.

Instruction Checking Questions: "What will you be doing in this breakout room?" "What is the aim of making a balanced critique?" "How will this improve your persuasive skills?"

Concept Checking Questions: "What is the definition of constructive criticism?" "Why is positive feedback important?" "How do you provide a balanced critique?"

Teacher prompt for Open Class Feedback: "How did your perspectives change after hearing other news agencies' arguments and critiques? How would you use this feedback to improve your argument in a real-world scenario?"

Practice Opportunities for Students: Development of critical thinking skills, constructive criticism, improved reflection skills, active listening.

Teaching Meaning, Form, and Pronunciation: Here, the teacher can model constructive criticism while checking on students' understanding of this concept. The teacher can also work on checking that students are using language that is balanced and positive, in order to facilitate constructive feedback. The teacher can check on students' pronunciation and fluency as they offer feedback to their peers.
resource for a PowerPoint presentation game to utilise the balloon debate for B2 level adult students

Here's a sample game that you could include in a PowerPoint presentation to utilize the balloon debate for B2 level adult students:

Game: "Last One Standing"

Overview: In this game, students will take turns representing their assigned news agency and giving a persuasive argument as to why they should be the last to be saved from being dropped off the balloon. The goal is to be the last one standing and be crowned the winner of the game.

Equipment: Timer, list of news agencies, props such as a virtual balloon.

Rules:

1. Divide students into teams (e.g., BBC, Fox News, Al-Jazeera, CNN)
2. Participants will have 1 minute to make a persuasive argument as to why their news agency should be saved from being dropped off the balloon last.
3. The rest of the students in their team will judge the argument and raise their hand to vote if they found the argument convincing.
4. If the argument receives a unanimous vote from the team, that student will advance to the next round.
5. If the argument does not receive a unanimous vote, that student is out of the game.
6. The game continues in this manner until there is only one student left, who is crowned the winner of the game.

Instructions: The PowerPoint presentation can be set up to include virtual balloons or a timer (e.g., 1-minute countdown clock) to indicate when each student's time is up. You can also incorporate images of each news agency's logo and an easy-to-understand voting system using virtual hands.

As the game progresses, the PowerPoint presentation can be updated to eliminate students who have been voted off and follow the progress of the game. The presentation can be concluded with announcing the winner and a reflection on persuasive arguments that game participants found to be compelling.

This game can help students improve their persuasive skills, active listening, and critical thinking through constructive feedback, debate and competition.
graphic examples of the game to input to presentation that can be found online

Here are some online graphic examples that you can use for the game in your PowerPoint presentation:

1. Balloon Images: You can find free balloon images on websites like Freepik or Pexels that can be used to create fun visuals for the game.

2. Hand Icons: You can use hand icons to represent the voting system in the game. These can be found on websites like Iconfinder or Flaticon.

3. News Agency Logos: Each news agency that students represent in the game should have their logo represented in the PowerPoint presentation. These can be found by doing a simple Google search or visiting the website of the news agency in question.

4. Progress Bar: You can create a progress bar to show the progress of the game in the PowerPoint presentation. This can be created by using shapes or progress bar templates in PowerPoint.

5. Timer: There are many free timer templates available on websites like Slidesgo or Template.net that you can customize and insert into the presentation.

It's important to ensure that all images and graphics used in the presentation are of high quality and can be used freely and legally. Make sure to follow the licensing guidelines for each image and ensure that you credit the original source if required.
